title: Word Sums, Matrices, and Suffixing Conventions
subtitle: The Tools of Structured Word Inquiry
summary: Provides hands-on practice with the core representational tools of SWI: word sums, matrices, and suffixing conventions. Teachers learn to write and verify word sums, build and read matrices, and apply the four join conventions (Replace, Double, Change, Toggle) using the 'Check the Joins!' framework.
learning_outcomes: Write accurate word sums using standard notation (base + affix → word)
Verify word sums by testing for shared sense across a word family
Build and read matrices as generative maps of word families
Apply the four suffixing conventions: Replace, Double, Change, Toggle
Use the 'Check the Joins!' framework to analyze what happens when morphemes meet
Identify when <-es> is an orthographic outcome of <-s>, not a separate suffix
key_concepts: Suffixing convention: Replace (final non-syllabic <e> replaced by vowel suffix)
Suffixing convention: Double (final consonant doubled before vowel suffix)
Suffixing convention: Change (letter changes per orthographic convention)
Suffixing convention: Toggle (predictable swap tied to convention)
Shared sense as the test for word family membership
swi_connections: Word sums make Question 2 (How is it built?) visible and testable
Matrices operationalize Question 3 (What other words are related?)
Join conventions connect structure to spelling at the morpheme boundary
All tools connect back to meaning as the anchor
int_connections: Word sums and matrices make structure visible, reducing hidden processing
Named conventions (Replace, Double, Change, Toggle) replace vague 'rules'
Predictable tools support executive function and working memory
Explicit join language avoids confusing framings like 'drop the e'

sections.title: Reading and writing word sums
sections.summary: Standard word sum notation: morpheme + morpheme → word. Reading a word sum aloud ('play plus ing makes playing'). What the + marks (a morpheme boundary) and what the → represents. First examples across all morpheme positions: un+do, help+ful+ness, re+construct+ion. The word sum as a claim about structure, not a display of spelling.

sections.title: A word sum is a hypothesis
sections.summary: Writing a word sum proposes a morphological analysis — it does not assert one. The verification procedure: does each proposed element carry its established meaning in this word? Testing <dis+cover>: does <dis-> mean 'not' or 'apart from' here? When a proposed sum fails the sense test, and what that reveals about the word's actual structure.

sections.title: The matrix: generative map of a word family
sections.summary: A matrix organizes prefixes, the base, and suffixes into a grid from which valid word sums can be generated by reading across any row. Building a matrix from the base <play>: adding prefixes (re+, dis+) and suffixes (+ed, +er, +ful, +ing, +mate). Reading the matrix to generate and verify the complete word family. What a matrix makes visible that a word list cannot.

sections.title: When morphemes meet — Replace
sections.summary: When a vowel suffix follows a base ending in a non-syllabic <e>, the <e> is replaced: <make+ing→making>, <use+able→usable>, <hope+ed→hoped>. Why 'replaced' is more accurate than 'dropped': the <e> served a function and the suffix takes it over. Consonant suffixes do not trigger Replace: <hope+ful→hopeful>, <use+less→useless>.

sections.title: When morphemes meet — Double
sections.summary: Doubling a final consonant before a vowel suffix when the base ends in a single vowel grapheme + single consonant in a stressed syllable: <run+ing→running>, <begin+er→beginner>, <sit+ing→sitting>. The conditions that block doubling: two vowel graphemes (<look+ing→looking>), unstressed final syllable (<open+ing→opening>), consonant suffix (<run+s→runs>).

sections.title: When morphemes meet — Change and Toggle
sections.summary: Change covers <y→i> when a consonant suffix follows a base ending in <y>: <happy+er→happier>, <carry+ed→carried>. Note: <y> does not change before vowel suffixes (<carry+ing→carrying>). Toggle covers predictable alternations such as <f→v> in plural formation: <leaf+s→leaves>, <wolf+s→wolves>. Both are named, principled conventions — not arbitrary spelling changes.

sections.title: <-es> as an orthographic outcome, not a separate suffix
sections.summary: When <-s> follows a base ending in a sibilant, the surface spelling is <-es>: <match+s→matches>, <church+s→churches>, <buzz+s→buzzes>. The <-es> is not a distinct suffix — it is the orthographic result of adding <-s> to that phonological environment. The morpheme is still <-s>; the spelling adapts. The same logic applies to third-person singular verb forms.

sections.title: Check the Joins!: a complete analysis
sections.summary: The Check the Joins! framework as a systematic checklist for every morpheme boundary in a word. Applying Replace, Double, Change, and Toggle in sequence. Full worked examples: <un+believe/+able→unbelievable> (Replace), <con+troll+able→controllable> (Double), <happi+ness> (Change). Practice analysis of unfamiliar words using the framework.
